seo.title: AP Psych Curve 2026 | AP Psychology Score Conversion Explained
seo.description: Learn how the AP Psych curve works and how raw MCQ and FRQ scores may convert to an estimated AP Psychology score from 1 to 5.

content.directAnswer: The AP Psych curve is best understood as an estimated raw-to-scaled score conversion. A higher MCQ total and stronger FRQ score both raise your composite estimate, but actual AP score cutoffs can vary by year and exam form.

sections.heading: What Is the AP Psych Curve?
sections.body: Students often call the score conversion a curve, but the practical question is how raw exam performance turns into a 1 to 5 score. For planning, treat the curve as an estimated composite range rather than a fixed number of questions you must answer correctly.

sections.heading: Raw Score to AP Score Conversion
sections.body: Your MCQ correct count and FRQ points combine into a composite estimate. For example, a student with a strong MCQ score but weaker FRQs may land near a cutoff, while a student with fewer MCQ points can sometimes recover with stronger FRQ performance.

sections.heading: Estimated Score Band Scenarios
sections.body: As a rough planning model, a composite estimate around the mid-70s or higher is usually a safer 5 target, around the 60s can point toward a 4, and around the mid-40s or higher can point toward a 3. Use the AP Psych Score Calculator to test your own MCQ and FRQ mix.

sections.heading: How to Use the Curve Without Overtrusting It
sections.body: Use the curve page to understand the relationship between raw performance and score bands, then use the calculator to test scenarios. Do not treat any estimated cutoff as guaranteed, because official scoring decisions and exam forms can change.

faqs.question: Is this calculator official?
faqs.answer: No. This calculator is an independent estimate and is not affiliated with or endorsed by College Board.

faqs.question: Can actual AP score cutoffs vary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Actual score thresholds can vary by year and exam form, so any curve estimate should be used for planning only.

faqs.question: Is the AP Psych curve the same every year?
faqs.answer: No. Students often look for one fixed curve, but estimated cutoffs can shift. That is why scenario testing is safer than relying on a single raw-score number.

faqs.question: Does FRQ performance affect the AP Psych curve?
faqs.answer: Yes. FRQ performance affects your composite estimate, so it can change where you land within an estimated score band.

faqs.question: What is a safe composite target for a 5?
faqs.answer: A composite estimate around the mid-70s or higher is a safer planning target for a 5, but it is still an estimate rather than an official cutoff.

faqs.question: Should I use the curve before or after the exam?
faqs.answer: Use it before the exam to set targets and after the exam to estimate possible outcomes. In both cases, compare multiple MCQ and FRQ scenarios.
